name: OpenAI Operator
description: OpenAI Operator is a browser-based autonomous agent using the Computer-Using Agent (CUA) model to navigate websites via vision-based screen understanding. Launched Jan 2025 for ChatGPT Pro, it completes multi-step web tasks without site-specific integrations. CUA API available via Responses API for programmatic browser automation.
oneLiner: OpenAI's autonomous browser agent powered by the CUA model that navigates websites through vision-based screen interaction.
pricing: ChatGPT Pro ($200/mo) includes Operator; CUA API: $1.50/$6.00 per 1M input/output tokens; Plus/Team/Enterprise also have access

screenshots.alt: OpenAI Operator — browser agent interface navigating a website autonomously
OpenAI CUA — Computer-Using Agent model architecture diagram

capabilities: Autonomous browser navigation via CUA model
User auth pass-through with login pausing
CUA API via Responses API for programmatic use
Hybrid with function calling, file search, code interpreter
Agent Builder visual canvas with drag-and-drop nodes
Built-in Guardrails node (PII, Moderation, Jailbreak, Hallucination)
Automated eval with trace grading and prompt optimization

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: Consumer: type task in ChatGPT Pro, Operator opens browser and executes. Watch in real-time, intervene when prompted. API: send screenshots to CUA model, receive action responses, implement own browser loop with Playwright. No visual builder, no agent config UI.
strengths: Universal web access without integration work
Intuitive: describe task in English, watch it happen
Strong HITL safety: auto-pauses for sensitive actions
Visual audit trail with screenshot replay
CUA API enables custom browser automation products
weaknesses: No enterprise agent builder or orchestration platform
Browser-only: no desktop apps, terminals, or non-web systems
Fragile to UI changes on target websites
High latency per action (screenshot + API + execution)
No multi-agent, templates, or reusable components
No RAG or proprietary data grounding
notableFeatures: CUA model: purpose-trained for browser via RL on web tasks
Auto sensitive-action detection with user prompts
Responses API: CUA + function calling + file search in one workflow
Vision-grounded actions from screen content, not DOM parsing

recentDevelopments: 2026: Agent Builder visual canvas in beta — drag-and-drop nodes, guardrails, versioning, eval config
2026: AgentKit launched — production framework for building agents
2026: Connector Registry beta rollout to Enterprise and Edu customers
2026: Operator fully integrated into ChatGPT as 'agent mode' — standalone Operator site sunsetting
2026: Expanded eval capabilities — datasets, trace grading, automated prompt optimization, third-party model support
2026: Codex adds Realtime V2 with background agent streaming
Jan 2025: Operator launched for ChatGPT Pro ($200/mo)
Jan 2025: CUA model introduced (GPT-4o vision + RL)
Mar 2025: CUA API via Responses API for developers
2025: CUA + function calling + file search hybrid workflows

deploymentOptions.notes: Cloud-only in OpenAI's sandbox. No self-hosted. All execution on OpenAI infrastructure.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: No builder UI. Consumer is prompt-driven. API requires building own orchestration loop.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: No debugging suite. Manual screenshot inspection. No trace visualization or testing framework.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Good OpenAI API docs. CUA guides. Lacks enterprise patterns and production best practices.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: Rapid prompt iteration. But testing requires full browser sessions (minutes per run).

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: No multi-agent. Single agent per session. Must compose externally.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: Basic but effective: works, pauses, human intervenes. No structured workflow tools.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: No team features. No shared configs, version control, or collaboration.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: No templates or reusable workflow patterns. Start from prompt each time.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: No pre-built connectors. Browser automation provides breadth but not structured depth.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: CUA via Responses API. Well-documented REST. Tool-use protocol. No MCP. Wide SDK adoption.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: Standard API keys. No per-agent credentials, secret rotation, or OAuth delegation.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: No RAG. Can combine CUA with file search and code interpreter for hybrid workflows.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: User can stop session. Content policy guardrails. No enterprise circuit breakers.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: Strong built-in HITL pausing for sensitive actions. Binary approve/deny, no multi-level.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: Screenshot audit trail. SOC 2 Type II. No tamper-proof storage or compliance dashboards.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: Basic org/project access. No Operator-specific RBAC or per-agent scoping.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: Cloud-only in OpenAI's sandbox. No self-hosted. Screenshots sent to OpenAI cloud.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: CUA API allows concurrent sessions. Browser resource overhead limits scale. Rate limits apply.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: No production monitoring dashboard. Standard API usage metrics only.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: Largest AI developer community. ChatGPT 200M+ WAU. Massive brand recognition.

scoringRationale: 52/100 overall. Strongest: Operations (13/20). Weakest: Orchestration (6/20). Breakdown: Creation (10/20), Orchestration (6/20), Integration (11/20), Governance (12/20), Operations (13/20).
targetUser: Users automating web-based tasks via visual AI

communityBuzz: Operator now integrated into ChatGPT as agent mode — standalone interface consolidated
CUA browser agent praised for zero-integration approach but fragile to UI changes
Expensive vision tokens per task is the main cost concern
87% WebVoyager benchmark gets attention but real-world reliability is lower

walkthrough.title: OpenAI Operator: PM & CTO Walkthrough — ChatGPT Agent Builder

steps.title: Guardrails Node — Safety Controls
steps.description: Guardrails node: PII detection, Moderation, Jailbreak prevention, Hallucination checks. Each with toggle and config. Connected to Agent → File Search → End flow.

works: 4 safety checks as toggles — PII, Moderation, Jailbreak, Hallucination — simplest governance UX ever
Guardrails as a visual node means safety is part of the flow, not an afterthought
Each check has a settings gear — configurable severity and behavior
doesntWork: Guardrails is between agent and end — not before agent input, creating a gap
'Continue on error' toggle suggests guardrails can be bypassed
No custom guardrail creation visible — limited to 4 built-in types
pmCritique.userPsychology: This is the frame that will be in every enterprise AI presentation for the next 2 years. Visual guardrails as drag-and-drop nodes makes AI safety tangible and manageable.

ctoCritique.verdict: This single node solves my #1 concern about deploying AI agents. PII detection + jailbreak prevention + hallucination checks as built-in toggles is what every other platform should copy.
concerns: Guardrails at the end of flow means agent processes before safety check
Custom guardrails not supported — only 4 built-in types
enterpriseGaps: No custom guardrail creation for industry-specific rules

steps.title: Agent Builder Canvas
steps.description: Visual canvas with MCP, Start, Agent (My agent) nodes. Right panel: Name, Instructions, Model gpt-5, Reasoning effort low, Tools, Output format, Evaluate link.

works: GPT-5 as default model — frontier capability out of the box
Reasoning effort selector (low/medium/high) enables cost-quality trade-offs
Add context button on instructions — RAG integration inline
doesntWork: Instructions textarea is small for complex agent behavior
'You are a helpful assistant' default is generic — no industry templates
MCP node connected but configuration not visible
pmCritique.userPsychology: The canvas feels like Figma for AI agents — clean, professional, intuitive. The dark theme with colored nodes creates clear visual hierarchy.

ctoCritique.verdict: GPT-5 with reasoning effort control is genuinely novel. Low effort for simple tasks, high for complex reasoning — this is the cost optimization lever I need.
concerns: Complete OpenAI lock-in — no alternative model providers
Reasoning effort impact on cost not quantified
enterpriseGaps: No cost estimation per reasoning level
No model comparison for same task

steps.title: Email Categorizer Agent
steps.description: Agent configured as Email Categorizer: Instructions classify emails as Urgent/Normal/Spam. Model gpt-5, Reasoning effort low, Output format Text.

works: Real-world use case — email categorization is universally relatable
Low reasoning effort for classification — smart cost optimization
Output format selection (Text, JSON, etc.) enables structured output
doesntWork: Classification prompt is verbose but has a typo ('standart') — no spell check
No example inputs/outputs for testing the categorization
Single agent — no pipeline to act on the categorization
pmCritique.userPsychology: Email categorization as the demo use case is brilliant — every enterprise has this problem. The simplicity makes evaluation effortless.

ctoCritique.verdict: Email categorization is a good starter use case but trivial. I need to see: multi-step workflows, external API calls, human approval chains, error handling.
concerns: Trivial use case doesn't stress-test the platform
No integration with actual email systems shown
enterpriseGaps: No email system connector (Gmail, Outlook, Exchange)

steps.title: Agent Flow with Deploy Pipeline
steps.description: Search agent → End node. Top bar: ... (menu), Evaluate, Code, Preview, Deploy buttons — full lifecycle in one toolbar.

works: Evaluate → Code → Preview → Deploy is the cleanest lifecycle pipeline in any agent builder
Each stage is a button, not a separate page — minimal context switching
Deploy button is green and prominent — clear call to action
doesntWork: Simple 2-node flow (agent → end) doesn't demonstrate real complexity
No error handling nodes visible — what happens when the agent fails?
No monitoring after deploy — lifecycle ends at deploy
pmCritique.userPsychology: The 4-button pipeline (Evaluate → Code → Preview → Deploy) is the UX pattern every agent builder should adopt. It makes the path from build to production explicit.

ctoCritique.verdict: The deploy pipeline is correct but incomplete. I need: staging environments, canary deployments, rollback, and post-deploy monitoring. Deploy is not the end of the lifecycle.
concerns: No staging environment before production deploy
enterpriseGaps: No deploy history or audit trail

steps.title: Code View — Generated Code
steps.description: Code tab showing generated Python/JavaScript from the visual builder. Evaluate, Code, Preview, Deploy tabs across the top.

works: Code generation from visual builder bridges no-code and developer workflows
Generated code is exportable — not locked into the visual builder
Developers can review, modify, and version control the output
doesntWork: Generated code quality and readability not shown in this frame
Code modifications don't sync back to visual builder
No testing framework for the generated code
pmCritique.userPsychology: Code view is the enterprise escape hatch — start visual, export to code when you outgrow the builder. This prevents builder lock-in.

ctoCritique.verdict: Code generation means we can version control, review in PRs, and deploy via our CI/CD pipeline. This is the bridge between no-code teams and engineering.
concerns: Code ↔ visual sync is one-way — code changes don't update the canvas
Generated code dependencies and runtime requirements unclear
No SDK for extending generated code
enterpriseGaps: No CI/CD integration for code-generated agents
No code quality analysis on generated output
No code migration between agent versions

steps.title: Publish Agent Workflow
steps.description: Deploy button and publication flow for pushing the agent workflow to production. Final step in the Evaluate → Code → Preview → Deploy pipeline.

works: One-click deploy is the culmination of the build experience
Deploy is contextual — only available after successful preview
Publication feels like shipping code — familiar mental model
doesntWork: No deployment target selection visible — where does it deploy?
No deployment confirmation or summary of what's being published
pmCritique.userPsychology: Deploy is the 'ship it' moment. The fact that it feels natural and unstressful means the builder has done its job leading up to this point.

ctoCritique.verdict: One-click deploy is great for demos. Production needs: approval gates, staged rollout, health checks, and automatic rollback on errors.

pmVerdict.summary: OpenAI's Agent Builder is the most polished visual agent builder in the market. The canvas with Agent, Guardrails, MCP, If/else, While nodes creates professional-grade workflows. Built-in Guardrails node (PII, Moderation, Jailbreak, Hallucination) is a CTO's dream. GPT-5 as the default model with reasoning effort control shows frontier capability. The Evaluate → Code → Preview → Deploy pipeline is best-in-class.
strengths: Most polished visual agent builder — clean dark canvas with professional node design
Built-in Guardrails node with PII, Moderation, Jailbreak, Hallucination toggles
GPT-5 default with reasoning effort control (low/medium/high)
Evaluate → Code → Preview → Deploy pipeline covers full lifecycle
risks: Complete OpenAI lock-in — no model provider choice beyond GPT models
Agent Builder is new — maturity and reliability unproven at scale
Pricing unclear — usage-based on GPT-5 could be expensive
No self-hosted option — all data flows through OpenAI

ctoVerdict.summary: OpenAI Agent Builder is the most compelling new entrant. Guardrails as a first-class node (PII detection, jailbreak prevention, hallucination checks) solves the #1 enterprise concern about AI agents. The visual builder with Code view enables both business users and developers. But: complete OpenAI lock-in, no self-hosted option, and GPT-5 pricing at scale are blockers for cost-sensitive enterprises.
strengths: Guardrails node with PII/Moderation/Jailbreak/Hallucination is enterprise-ready out of the box
Visual builder + Code view serves both no-code and developer users
GPT-5 with reasoning effort control enables cost-quality optimization
1.7M+ views on intro video signals massive developer interest
dealBreakers: GPT-5 pricing at scale could be prohibitive
New product — production track record doesn't exist yet

coverageGaps: Integration with external systems beyond MCP

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Visual canvas builder, node types, agent config, Guardrails all demonstrated
scoringAlignment.gap: Score should be higher — best visual builder in market

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: If/else, While logic nodes, multi-agent flows with MCP shown
scoringAlignment.gap: Complex orchestration patterns not deeply explored

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: MCP node, File Search tool shown. Limited external integrations
scoringAlignment.gap: Integration breadth limited to OpenAI ecosystem

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Guardrails node with 4 safety checks is the strongest governance UX in any platform
scoringAlignment.gap: RBAC, audit trails, compliance certifications not shown

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Deploy button visible, Evaluate tab shown
scoringAlignment.gap: Monitoring, scaling, cost management not demonstrated

youtubeReviews.title: NEW ChatGPT Agent Builder: From Zero to Automation Hero (2026)

coreCompetence.bestAt: The most capable browser-based AI agent — uses CUA (Computer Use Agent) model to navigate any website, fill forms, and complete web-based tasks autonomously.
coreCompetence.targetUser: Consumers and business users who want AI to handle web-based tasks: booking, purchasing, form filling, research. Early adopters exploring browser automation.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins on raw capability — OpenAI model is the most capable at web navigation. Wins on brand trust and ecosystem. Loses on enterprise features — no governance, audit trails, or deployment flexibility. Cloud-only, no self-hosted option.
